### Post by OliverN on 2008-08-12
I'm currently dualbooting Vista/Fedora and looking to add Ubuntu 8.04 to my grub menu.lst manually. Ubuntu has already been installed, but since Grub was already installed with Fedora, I decided not to install it again for Ubuntu.

I should be able to boot Ubuntu from (Fedora's) grub, right? My menu.lst (loading from my Fedora partition) looks like this as of now

```
# grub.conf generated by anaconda
#
# Note that you do not have to rerun grub after making changes to this file
# NOTICE:  You do not have a /boot partition.  This means that
#          all kernel and initrd paths are relative to /, eg.
#          root (hd0,0)
#          kernel /boot/vmlinuz-version ro root=/dev/sdc1
#          initrd /boot/initrd-version.img
#boot=/dev/sdc
default=1
timeout=5
splashimage=(hd0,0)/boot/grub/splash.xpm.gz
hiddenmenu
title Fedora 9
	root (hd0,0)
	kernel /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.25-14.fc9.x86_64 ro root=UUID=6086d77f-107e-4a9d-b491-0fa6f25c9754 rhgb quiet
	initrd /boot/initrd-2.6.25-14.fc9.x86_64.img
title Windows Vista
	rootnoverify (hd1,0)
	chainloader +1
title Ubuntu 8.04
	root (hd0,2)
	kernel /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic ro root=dev/sdc3
	initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic
	boot
```

The last entry is my own creation, but this doesn't work. Obviously I'm no pro at grub. How do I retrieve some working boot information for my menu.lst?

**I don't know if my partition table holds any relevance, but here it is:**

hd0,0 - Fedora
hd0,1 - Swap
hd0,2 - Ubuntu

hd1,0 Vista

hd2,0 Storage space (NTFS)

**As seen from the Ubuntu Live CD (GParted):**

dev/sda1 - Storage space (NTFS)

dev/sdb1 - Vista

dev/sdc1 - Fedora
dev/sdc2 - Swap
dev/sdc3 - Ubuntu

### Post by sandysandy on 2008-08-12
the menu.lst file should exist.

its just not linked to IPL (initial program loader)

see under Places ---> computer ------>Filesystem------>boot----> grub

### Post by mannheim on 2008-08-12
You seem to have typed something wrong in your menu.lst. You have:
```
title Ubuntu 8.04
	root (hd0,2)
	kernel /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic ro root=dev/sdc3
	initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic
	boot
```

In the "kernel" line, you probably need a forward slash in front of dev, like this:

```
kernel /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic ro root=/dev/sdc3
```

and the initrd line should be something like:

```
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic
```

---

	initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic

and the initrd line should be something like:

Code:

### Post by mannheim on 2008-08-12
I think a better way to do this is to first install the grub package in Ubuntu. This should not disturb your Fedora grub installation (but I may be wrong, please note!). I think that if you do:

```
sudo apt-get install grub
```

then you will have a menu.lst file installed and configured in /boot/grub/. Furthermore, it will be automatically updated with every kernel update. Then what you want to do is to get Fedora's grub menu to "chain" to the Ubuntu menu. To do this, you need to add a stanza like this to the Fedora version of menu.lst:

```
title           Ubuntu boot menu on sdc3
savedefault
configfile      (hd0,2)/boot/grub/menu.lst

```

Now, if you boot up and get to the Fedora grub screen, you will see an entry for "Ubuntu boot menu on sdc3". Selecting this menu item will get you to the menu options for booting Ubuntu.

---

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-08-12
I hope you don't mind me popping into this discussion, OliverN, but I would definitely recommend mannheim's suggestion and go ahead and install Grub in Ubuntu. That way your menu.lst will get automatically updated with new kernel upgrades. 

And I just wanted to mention that about the worst thing that could happen when you install Grub in Ubuntu is if you accidentally install Grub to the MBR, so that Fedora no longer controls the booting process (like you want it too). But to give back Fedora's Grub control of the MBR takes no longer than 30 seconds in the Grub CLI, and everything will be back to just the way you want it. So I'm just trying to emphasize, you really don't need to worry about installing Grub in Ubuntu. :)

---

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-08-12
I believe you have to run grub-install to actually generate all the config files in /boot/grub, and then run update-grub to create a menu.lst. Run these commands in Ubuntu:
```
sudo grub-install /dev/sdc3
sudo update-grub
```

---

### Post by OliverN on 2008-08-12
> **caljohnsmith said:**
> I believe you have to run grub-install to actually generate all the config files in /boot/grub, and then run update-grub to create a menu.lst. Run these commands in Ubuntu:
```
sudo grub-install /dev/sdc3
sudo update-grub
```

That did it (the default menu.lst set the root drives to 2,2 but that was easily corected).
